reason: In English, students 'take' exams, while teachers 'give' them.

wrong: I saw a dream last night.
right: I had a dream last night.
reason: Dreams are not 'seen' visually in English syntax, they are experienced or 'had'.
The word 'cousin' does not require a gender marker like brother or sister.
'Agree' is a verb on its own. You don't need 'am' before it.
'Discuss' implies 'talking about', so adding 'about' is redundant.
We use the preposition 'at' when talking about skills or abilities.
After 'does', the base form of the verb ('have') must be used.

wrong: I will call you when I will arrive.
right: I will call you when I arrive.
reason: In time clauses (after when, as soon as), use present simple, not future.
'Every' is singular, so it takes a singular noun and a singular verb.
'Advice' is an uncountable noun. It cannot take 'an' or be pluralized.
The verb 'listen' is always followed by the preposition 'to'.
'Reply' is intransitive and requires the preposition 'to' before the object.

wrong: She is married with a doctor.
right: She is married to a doctor.
reason: In English, a person is married 'to' someone, not 'with' them.
Mistakes are 'made', not 'done'. This is a common collocation error.
We use the preposition 'to' after 'prefer' when comparing two things.

wrong: He can able to do it.
reason: 'Can' and 'able to' mean the exact same thing; using both is redundant.
The hair on your head is considered an uncountable noun in English.
'Home' acts as an adverb of place here, so the preposition 'to' is not needed.
The verb 'practice' is always followed by a gerund (verb + -ing).

wrong: I have visited London last weekend.
reason: Use simple past, not present perfect, when a specific past time ('last weekend') is mentioned.
The word 'despite' does not take 'of'. You can say 'in spite of', but not 'despite of'.

wrong: What is the time in your watch?
right: What is the time by your watch?
reason: The correct preposition to use when asking the time using a watch is 'by'.

wrong: One of my friend is here.
right: One of my friends is here.
reason: The phrase 'one of' must always be followed by a plural noun.

wrong: I look forward to meet you.
right: I look forward to meeting you.
reason: In the phrase 'look forward to', 'to' is a preposition, so it must be followed by a gerund (-ing).
'Return' already means 'to give back', making 'back' redundant.
